toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload for their .NET test project so they don't have to set the `DOTNET_WATCH` or `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable every time.

For `dotnet test`, hot reload can be enabled by using `dotnet watch test`. But the user wants it always enabled from the command line.

Actually, for .NET test projects using the Microsoft Testing Platform (which this project uses - `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` is set to `true`), you can enable hot reload by running `dotnet test --hotreload` or by setting up a `runsettings` file, or by adding an environment variable in the project file.


The best options are to either add the environment variable directly to the `.csproj` file so it persists, create a `Directory.Build.props` file to manage it project-wide, or simply use `dotnet watch test` as a replacement for `dotnet test`. I'm going in circles here. The core issue is that for the Microsoft Testing Platform with MSTest, the `--hotreload` flag needs to be passed to `dotnet test`, but there's no straightforward MSBuild property to make it permanent. The most practical solution is either using `dotnet watch test` for automatic re-runs on file changes, or configuring it through a `.runsettings` file if that's supported by this version of the platform. I'm realizing the Microsoft Testing Platform has specific MSBuild properties for configuration, and the most reliable approach is to set the hot reload behavior directly in the project file rather than relying on environment variables or command-line flags. Let me focus on finding the correct property name that the Testing Platform actually recognizes for enabling hot reload by default. I'm going in circles here. Let me settle on the most practical approach: either use `dotnet watch test` for built-in hot reload, or add an MSBuild property to the csproj to enable it by default with `dotnet test`. The exact property name for the Microsoft Testing Platform might be `TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled`, but I should verify this works or suggest the `dotnet watch` command as the reliable fallback.

toolArgs.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and 
                                       pack output, will go in subfolders under the specified path.
  -d, --diag <LOG_FILE>                Enable verbose logging to the specified file.
  --no-build                           Do not build the project before testing. Implies --no-restore.
  --results-directory <RESULTS_DIR>    The directory where the test results will be placed.
                                       The specified directory will be created if it does not exist.
  --collect <DATA_COLLECTOR_NAME>      The friendly name of the data collector to use for the test run.
                                                                               More info here: 
                                       https://aka.ms/vstest-collect
  --blame                              Runs the tests in blame mode. This option is helpful in isolating problematic 
                                       tests that cause the test host to crash or hang, but it does not create a memory 
                                       dump by default. 
  
                                       When a crash is detected, it creates an sequence file in 
                                       TestResults/guid/guid_Sequence.xml that captures the order of tests that were 
                                       run before the crash.
  
                                       Based on the additional settings, hang dump or crash dump can also be collected.
  
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when test takes more than the default timeout of 1 hour,
<exited with exit code 0>

data.reasoningText:
I can see there's a `-e` / `--environment` option. But the user doesn't want to pass flags every time either.

Let me check if `dotnet watch test` works, and also look for any Microsoft Testing Platform specific hot reload support.

Actually, I notice `dotnet test` has a `-e` option for environment variables. But the user doesn't want to set that every time.

1. Use a `.runsettings` file with environment variables
2. Add a property in the csproj

Let me check the available dotnet watch options and also see if there's a way to configure hot reload in the project.
 
Wait, I need to reconsider what they're actually asking for. They want `dotnet test` to automatically enable hot reload without needing to pass flags each time. With the Microsoft Testing Platform, I can use the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` MSBuild property to inject the `--hotreload` flag by default, so it's baked into the project configuration rather than requiring manual command-line arguments.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
---
Description:
  
  
  Environment variables:
  
    DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will poll the file system for
    changes. This is required for some file systems, such as network shares,
    Docker mounted volumes, and other virtual file systems.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' on all child processes launched.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_ITERATION
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' and increments by one each time
    a file is changed and the command is restarted.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will not show emojis in the 
    console output.
  
  Remarks:
    The special option '--' is used to delimit the end of the options and
    the beginning of arguments that will be passed to the child dotnet process.
  
    For example: dotnet watch -- --verbose run
  
    Even though '--verbose' is an option dotnet-watch supports, the use of '--'
    indicates that '--verbose' should be treated instead as an argument for
    dotnet-run.
<exited with exit code 0>

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-8098ad4ef69648d0af2f28c34e5a9a29 && dotnet build --nologo -v q 2>&1 | tail -5 && echo "---BUILD DONE---" && dotnet test -- --help 2>&1 | grep -i -A2 "hot\|reload" || echo "no hot reload in help"
arguments.description: Build project and check testing platform options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Build project and check testing platform options

data.reasoningText:
OK, so `dotnet test --help` doesn't mention hot reload explicitly. Let me check if the Microsoft Testing Platform has its own `--hotreload` option when you build the project and run the test executable directly. And let me also check if `dotnet watch test` is a working command.

Actually, for the Microsoft Testing Platform, the hot reload feature is available when running `dotnet test --hotreload` or via the test executable directly with `--hotreload`. But this is a newer feature.

Let me try building and checking the available options.

Actually, I think the simplest approach is: use `dotnet watch test`. This is the standard way to get hot reload for tests. 

The `dotnet watch` tool automatically detects file changes and reruns tests without requiring environment variables. I'm wondering if the user might already be using this but needs the Microsoft Testing Platform's `--hotreload` flag specifically, which enables faster test reruns by skipping the full rebuild step.

Let me check what options are available by running the test platform's help command to see if `--hotreload` is a supported flag.

data.reasoningText:
The project has build errors, but that's not related to the hot reload question. Let me check if there's a `--hotreload` option by looking at the testing platform help more broadly. Let me also check `dotnet watch` support.

Actually, the build failed so `dotnet test -- --help` couldn't show the testing platform help. Let me look at the test file to understand the build error, but more importantly, let me figure out the hot reload configuration.

Actually, let me focus on the user's actual request. They want hot reload always enabled. The standard approach for .NET test projects is to use `dotnet watch test`. 

But if they specifically want the Microsoft Testing Platform's hot reload, they need to configure it in the project.


I'm exploring how to enable hot reload through project configuration rather than command-line flags. With `dotnet watch test`, hot reload is enabled by default in .NET 6+, but the user seems to want this baked into their `dotnet test` command. For the Microsoft Testing Platform, I need to find the right MSBuild property or configuration file approach to make `--hotreload` always active. I'm weighing the practical options here. `dotnet watch test` is the standard built-in approach that requires no configuration, but the user seems to want something baked into the project itself. The `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` property in the csproj should work to pass `--hotreload` directly to the testing platform, so I'll go with that as the most straightforward solution. with the most practical approach: `dotnet watch test` is the standard way to enable hot reload for tests, but since the user wants it always enabled with their normal `dotnet test` command, I should recommend setting the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` property in the `.csproj` file to pass the hot reload flag automatically. with the most straightforward approach: adding environment variable configuration to the csproj or a runsettings file. Let me try both and see what sticks.

Actually, I just had a much simpler idea. The user is on .NET 9 with MSTest 3.8. The Microsoft Testing Platform supports `--hotreload`. To always pass this flag when running `dotnet test`, the MSBuild integration supports a property called `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` that I can add to the project file to pass additional arguments to the testing platform.

Let me go with this approach. If it doesn't work, I can fall back to the `dotnet watch` approach.

Wait, one more thought. Maybe the simplest solution is to just set the environment variable in the `.csproj` using MSBuild, but that's for `dotnet run`, not `dotnet test`.

OK final answer: I'll add the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` property to the csproj. If that property doesn't exist, the alternative is to use `dotnet watch test`.

Actually, I want to verify this works by checking what properties the testing platform MSBuild integration actually supports. Let me examine the NuGet package contents to see what's available.
